content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
GaryH. Mayo 
1925 Otay Lakes Rd. Spc. 125 
Chula Vista, California 91913 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-168 
Dear Mr. Mayo: 
September 30, 2011 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 In our reply, we restrict our comments to the applicability of 
the Act to your question. 
You are the treasurer of the Chula Vista California Mobile Home Association, which 
intends to organize a political action committee (the "PAC"). The association would like to 
make a contribution of less than $1,000 to the PAC. You ask whether the Act prohibits this 
contribution. 
The provisions of the Political Reform Act governing political contributions are primarily 
disclosure rules. While there are certain restrictions and prohibitions on the making of 
contributions] none apply to your facts. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit the association 
from making a contribution to the PAC. 
However, we bring your attention to provisions of the Act that govern "committees," as 
defined in Section 82013. If the association receives contributions or makes expenditures 
totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year, it will be deemed to be a "committee" and must file a 
statement of organization within ten days and will be subject to the recordkeeping, reporting and 
other rules governing committees. Generally, once an organization has made its first political 
contributions or expenditures totaling $1,000 or more, the sources of any funds used by the 
organization to make those contributions must be reported. In other words, the association 
would be required to disclose, on a campaign report, the names and certain other information 
about individuals or organizations that contributed to the association. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 l O through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-11-168 
Page No. 2 
To assist the association in complying with these provisions, we are enclosing our 
pamphlet entitled "Candidate and Campaign Committees." 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me or our Technical Assistance 
Division at (916) 322-5660. 
VJ:jgl 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question: You are the treasurer of the Chula Vista California Mobile Home Association, which intends to organize a political action committee (the "PAC"). The association would like to make a contribution of less than $1,000 to the PAC. You ask whether the Act prohibits this contribution.
sections.conclusion: The provisions of the Political Reform Act governing political contributions are primarily disclosure rules. While there are certain restrictions and prohibitions on the making of contributions, none apply to your facts. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit the association from making a contribution to the PAC. However, if the association receives contributions or makes expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year, it will be deemed to be a "committee" and must file a statement of organization within ten days and will be subject to recordkeeping, reporting and other rules governing committees.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a nonprofit association from making a contribution of less than $1,000 to a political action committee it intends to organize.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit the association's contribution of less than $1,000 to the PAC; however, if the association's total contributions or expenditures reach $1,000 or more in a calendar year, it becomes a committee subject to disclosure and reporting requirements, including disclosure of contribution sources.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Dated September 30, 2011. Addressed to Gary H. Mayo, treasurer of Chula Vista California Mobile Home Association. File No. A-11-168.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You are the treasurer of the Chula Vista California Mobile Home Association, which intends to organize a political action committee (the "PAC"). The association would like to make a contribution of less than $1,000 to the PAC. You ask whether the Act prohibits this contribution.

CONCLUSION: The provisions of the Political Reform Act governing political contributions are primarily disclosure rules. While there are certain restrictions and prohibitions on the making of contributions, none apply to your facts. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit the association from making a contribution to the PAC. However, if the association receives contributions or makes expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year, it will be deemed to be a "committee" and must file a statement of organization within ten days and will be subject to recordkeeping, reporting and other rules governing committees.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit a mobile home association from contributing less than $1,000 to a political action committee, but warns that contributions or expenditures of $1,000 or more would trigger committee status and associated disclosure obligations.
